source_files: gut bacteria x vitamin status - Sources.csv

findings.title: Bifidobacterium as Folate Producers
findings.content: Folate biosynthesis is well-characterized in gut bacteria. Among 256 gut bacterial genomes assessed, 40-65% carry biosynthesis pathways for folate. Bifidobacteria are key producers, particularly B. adolescentis and B. pseudocatenulatum, which produce 41-82 ng/mL folate in folate-free medium. However, folate production is a strain-level trait, not genus-level. B. infantis RECb4 contains complete pathway genes but requires exogenous folate.

findings.title: Bifidobacterium as Folate Scavengers
findings.content: Some bifidobacteria that possess complete folate biosynthesis genes act as scavengers when exogenous folate is available, consuming rather than releasing folate. B. breve ATCC 15701 demonstrated scavenger phenotype in the presence of folate-containing media. Genomic potential does not always translate to net folate supply.

findings.title: PABA-Dependent Folate Synthesis
findings.content: Only 13% of 512 gastrointestinal reference genomes contain all genes for complete de novo folate synthesis. However, 39% can synthesize folate if supplied with para-aminobenzoic acid (pABA), an intermediate from other gut microbes or diet. This demonstrates the cooperative, cross-feeding nature of microbial vitamin metabolism.

findings.title: Microbial Folate Bioavailability
findings.content: Rat trials showed probiotic folate-producing bifidobacteria increased plasma folate levels. Human trials showed folate-producing supplements raised fecal folate. However, fecal folate does not directly demonstrate colonic absorption, and human data on meaningful systemic contribution remains limited.

findings.title: Cobalamin: Precious Resource Not Provisioned to Host
findings.content: Cobalamin biosynthesis requires ~30 enzymatic steps and is restricted to specific prokaryotes. Despite some gut microbes synthesizing B12, Degnan et al. argue it is a precious resource unlikely provisioned to the host in significant quantities. Most microbial B12 synthesis occurs in the colon, but B12 absorption occurs primarily in the ileum via intrinsic factor, creating a geographic mismatch that limits host benefit.

findings.title: Bacteroides as B12 Consumers, Not Producers
findings.content: Bacteroides are sophisticated B12 consumers, not producers. B. thetaiotaomicron encodes multiple outer membrane transport systems for B12, including surface-exposed lipoproteins (BtuG) that bind B12 with femtomolar affinity—strong enough to strip B12 from human intrinsic factor. BtuH proteins represent functionally distinct transport machines. This means Bacteroides may reduce B12 availability to the host rather than enhance it.

findings.title: Cobalamin as Ecological Modulator
findings.content: Competition for cobalamin shapes microbial community structure. Analysis of ~8,000 human gut metagenomes reveals cobalamin biosynthesis pathways play a key role in equilibrium establishment of adult gut communities, distinct from infant communities which are modulated by folate.

findings.title: Biotin Production Across Gut Bacteria
findings.content: Biotin is predicted to be produced by 40-65% of gut microbes according to genome survey. Rodionov et al. identified significant cross-feeding of biotin between species across 2,228 bacterial genomes. However, detailed experimental studies on Bifidobacterium or Bacteroides biotin production are limited.

findings.title: Vitamin K Biosynthesis in Gut Microbiota
findings.content: Vitamin K production is an important function of gut bacteria. Comprehensive assessment across 8,000 gut microbiomes shows vitamin K biosynthetic capacity varies with host-associated factors including age and geographical origin.

findings.title: Dietary Folate and Microbiota Composition
findings.content: In 35 men, colonic bacterial composition and richness differed significantly by dietary folate and B-vitamin intake. Lower consumption was associated with lower abundance of Akkermansia (folate), Roseburia (B2), and Faecalibacterium (B2, B6, B12), but higher abundance of Erysipelatoclostridium (B2).

findings.title: Causal Associations Between Microbiota and B12 Deficiency
findings.content: Mendelian randomization study of 8,000 individuals identified causal associations between specific gut microbiota features and B12 deficiency. Notably, Akkermansia and Verrucomicrobiae family taxa showed association, as did genera Coprococcus 2, Lactococcus, and Ruminococcaceae UCG002. Enterobacteriaceae and Rhodospirillaceae families also associated with B12 deficiency.

findings.title: Obesity-Associated Depletion of B12-Producing Genes
findings.content: Mouse obesity models show significantly lower predicted abundance of enzymes and metabolic pathways for B12 biosynthesis. Enzyme depletion was most pronounced in severe obesity (db/db mice). Degree of obesity and correspondent microbiota composition are main contributors to genes and pathways for cobalamin biosynthesis.

findings.title: Auxotrophic Species Dependency on B-Vitamin Cross-Feeding
findings.content: More than 20% of gut community abundance consists of auxotrophic species strictly dependent on acquiring B-vitamins from prototrophs or diet. Gnotobiotic mouse studies showed gut communities remain remarkably stable under extreme B-vitamin dietary variation (zero to ~30-fold excess), suggesting microbial B-vitamin sharing buffers communities.

findings.title: Estimated Microbial Contribution to Host B-Vitamin Status
findings.content: Gut microbiota estimated to contribute up to ~30% of recommended daily intake for B-vitamins depending on microbiome composition and diet. However, major caveat: most microbial synthesis occurs in the colon where absorption mechanisms for many vitamins (especially B12) are limited.

findings.title: CAUTION: Supplemental B12 May Alter Microbial Competition
findings.content: Supplemental B12 availability can shift microbial competition dynamics. As B12 is a precious resource tightly competed for by Bacteroides and other species, exogenous supplementation may select for high-affinity B12 consumers (like Bacteroides), potentially reducing availability to host or beneficial bacteria. The ecological consequence of chronic B12 supplementation remains understudied.

findings.title: Lactobacillus and Folate Biosynthesis
findings.content: Most wild-type lactobacilli cannot synthesize folate and require it for growth, providing negative contribution to folate levels. Exception: L. plantarum is capable of folate production in presence of pABA (para-aminobenzoic acid). L. reuteri modulates host folate receptors (RFC) when producing polyglutamylated folate forms.

findings.title: Other B-Vitamins in Gut Microbiota
findings.content: B-vitamin biosynthesis pathways exist across gut bacteria: riboflavin, niacin, pantothenate, pyridoxine, and thiamine are produced de novo by 40-65% of gut microbes. Some bacteria produce in excess, notable sources include riboflavin, niacin, and cobalamin producers. Systemic contribution varies significantly.

Magnúsdóttir_2015.title: Systematic genome assessment of B-vitamin biosynthesis suggests co-operation among gut microbes
Magnúsdóttir_2015.authors: Stefanía Magnúsdóttir, D. Ravcheev, V. de Crécy-Lagard, I. Thiele
key_findings: 40-65% of 256 gut bacteria have biosynthesis pathways for each of eight B-vitamins
Vitamin synthesis pathways are diverse and complementary
88% of genomic predictions match experimental data
Evidence for B-vitamin exchange between species
Magnúsdóttir_2015.relevance: Foundational survey of B-vitamin biosynthetic capacity across gut microbiota

Pompei_2006.title: Folate Production by Bifidobacteria as a Potential Probiotic Property
Pompei_2006.authors: A. Pompei, Lisa Cordisco, Alberto Amaretti, Simona Zanoni, D. Matteuzzi, M. Rossi
key_findings: Most of 76 Bifidobacterium strains required folate for growth
B. adolescentis and B. pseudocatenulatum produced 41-82 ng/mL folate
Folate production is strain-level, not species-level trait
B. adolescentis MB 239 showed consistent folate production across pH and carbon sources
Pompei_2006.relevance: Seminal work identifying folate-producing bifidobacterial strains

Potential for folate-producing probiotics in fermented products
Opportunity to address folate deficiency in vulnerable populations
D'Aimmo_2023.relevance: Recent comprehensive review of bifidobacterial folate metabolism

Celik_2022.title: Correlation of Gene Content in Selected Bifidobacteria with Folate Supplier or Scavenger Status During Growth in Laboratory Media
key_findings: Gene content cannot reliably predict folate phenotype in bifidobacteria
B. longum and B. breve can produce folate despite missing pathway genes
Some strains act as folate scavengers despite biosynthetic capacity
Critical to distinguish folate suppliers from scavengers for probiotic applications
Celik_2022.relevance: Reveals genotype-phenotype discordance in bifidobacterial folate metabolism

key_findings: 13% of 512 GI genomes contain complete de novo folate synthesis genes
39% can synthesize folate if supplied with pABA
L. reuteri-derived folate modulates RFC expression in colonoid monolayers
Folate polyglutamylation increases during stationary phase
Engevik_2019.relevance: Demonstrates bacterial folate availability depends on intermediate provisioning and host-microbe interactions

Rossi_2011.title: Folate Production by Probiotic Bacteria
Rossi_2011.authors: M. Rossi, Alberto Amaretti, S. Raimondi
key_findings: Rat trials: folate-producing bifidobacteria increased plasma folate
Human trials: same supplement raised fecal folate
B. adolescentis and B. pseudocatenulatum are strongest folate producers
L. plantarum can produce folate in presence of pABA
Rossi_2011.relevance: Early in vivo evidence for microbial folate bioavailability

Abavana.title: Cobamide Biosynthesis
key_findings: Cobalamin biosynthesis requires ~30 enzymatic steps
Only certain prokaryotes produce cobamides
Gut bacteria utilize cobamides for methionine synthesis and propionic acid production
Host cannot absorb B12 across large intestine despite microbial production
Abavana.relevance: Technical foundation for understanding B12 biosynthesis complexity

Degnan_2014.title: Vitamin B12 as a modulator of gut microbial ecology
Degnan_2014.authors: P. Degnan, M. Taga, A. Goodman
key_findings: B12 is a precious resource in gut, unlikely provisioned to host in significant quantities
Geographic mismatch: microbial B12 made in colon but absorbed in ileum
B12 competition shapes microbial community structure
B12 influences host-microbe symbioses
Degnan_2014.relevance: Seminal paper establishing B12 as ecological modulator, not host nutrient source

BtuG binds B12 with femtomolar affinity
Can strip B12 from human intrinsic factor
Bacteroides are consumers, not producers of B12
